        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing SPACE:
- The white strip (W) shrank from (27,40)-(31,43) [16 pixels] back to (27,40)-(27,43) [4 pixels]
- A new G object appeared at (28,40)-(31,43) [12 pixels] with blue center at (29,41)-(30,42)
So pressing SPACE detached the carried square from the player! The white strip shrank back to its original size, and the carried square (now G) is back at its original position (28,40)-(31,43).
Wait, but the carried square was at (40,40)-(43,43) before. Now it's at (28,40)-(31,43). That's different. Let me think about this.
Actually, looking at the earlier state:
- Before SPACE: white strip at (27,40)-(31,43) with blue center at (29,41)-(30,42)
- After SPACE: white strip at (27,40)-(27,43), G at (28,40)-(31,43) with blue center at (29,41)-(30,42)
So the white strip detached from the carried square, and the carried square is now at (28,40)-(31,43), which is where the white strip was (minus the top row).
So pressing SPACE toggles the attachment: when the player is adjacent to a square, SPACE attaches it; when the player is carrying a square and adjacent to another object, SPACE detaches it (or maybe merges it?).
Wait, but the carried square is still separate from the merged object. The merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47), and the carried square is at (28,40)-(31,43). They're adjacent but not merged.
Let me think about what the goal is. Maybe the goal is to merge all squares into one object. The merged object has 2 blue centers, and the left square has 1 blue center, and the carried square has 1 blue center. If I merge all of them, I'd have 4 blue centers in one object.
But I'm not sure how to merge them. Let me try a different approach.
Actually, looking at the image, the player is at (24,40)-(26,43), and the carried square is at (28,40)-(31,43). The merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). The left square is at (28,28)-(31,39).
The carried square is adjacent to both the merged object (right edge) and the left square (left edge). Maybe I need to position the player such that the carried square is between the merged object and the left square, then press SPACE to merge them.
But wait, the carried square is already between the merged object and the left square. The left square is at cols 28-39, the carried square is at cols 40-43, and the merged object is at cols 44-47. So the carried square is between the left square and the merged object.
Maybe I need to press SPACE while the carried square is adjacent to both objects. Let me try pressing SPACE again.
Actually, wait. Looking at the segmentation, the carried square (G at 28,40)-(31,43) is adjacent to the merged object (c at 24,44)-(31,47) at cols 43-44. And the carried square is adjacent to the left square (b at 28,28)-(31,39) at cols 39-40.
So the carried square is sandwiched between the left square and the merged object. Maybe pressing SPACE will merge all three objects.
Let me try pressing SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Carried square (G at 28,40)-(31,43) is between left square (28,28)-(31,39) and merged object (24,44)-(31,47)
# Press SPACE to merge
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
